avtpComponents.name: welcome_text
avtpComponents.text:
Welcome to the Reaction Time Task.

In this experiment, you will see a fixation cross (+) followed by a green circle.

Your task is to press the SPACEBAR as quickly as possible when you see the green circle.

Please keep your finger resting on the Spacebar. Try to be both fast and accurate.

Press 'Enter' to continue.

avtpComponents.name: main_instr_text
avtpComponents.text:
Practice complete. The main experiment will now begin.

There will be 50 trials. No feedback will be given during this phase.

Remember: Press the Spacebar as soon as you see the green circle.

Press 'Enter' to start.

avtpComponents.name: end_text
avtpComponents.text:
Experiment Complete.

Thank you for your participation.
Your data has been saved.

You may now remove your headphones and inform the experimenter.

Thinking completed

Here's a thinking process:

  1. Analyze User Input:
  • User Requirements: "test"
  • This is extremely vague. It just says "test". I need to interpret this as a placeholder or request for a standard/template psychological experiment design, or perhaps they want me to generate a generic but well-structured example based on common paradigms.
  • Given the prompt instructions, I should create a concise experiment description following the specified format, even with minimal input. I'll choose a classic, widely understood paradigm (e.g., Stroop Task or Go/No-Go) to demonstrate the structure, but I'll make it adaptable or explicitly note that it's a template/example since the input was just "test".
  • Actually, the prompt says "analyze the user's experiment requirements and generate a concise experiment description." Since the requirement is just "test", I will assume they want a standard, well-structured experimental design template/example that follows all the rules. I'll pick a common cognitive psychology task like the Stroop Task or Flanker Task as a concrete example, but I'll frame it so it clearly meets all requirements. Alternatively, I could ask for clarification, but the prompt says "generate a concise experiment description", so I'll proceed with a complete, ready-to-use design based on a classic paradigm, noting that it can be adapted.

Wait, the prompt says: "IMPORTANT: The Experiment Summary sentence will be used as the task name, so make it concise (under 50 characters), descriptive, and meaningful."
I'll create a summary under 50 chars: Experiment Summary: Cognitive Interference Assessment via Stroop Paradigm (48 chars) - good.

  1. Check Requirements against Prompt:
  • Search for relevant methodologies/best practices (internal knowledge)
  • Generate concise description including:
    • Experiment title and purpose (ONE-SENTENCE summary at start)
    • Detailed experimental design and procedure
    • Variables (IV, DV, CV)
    • Trial structure and sequence
    • Stimulus presentation details
    • Response collection methods
    • Loops, conditions, branching logic
    • Timing parameters
  • Must include: (1) Instruction for entire exp; (2) Practice Loop; (3) Instruction for Main Exp; (4) Main Exp Loop; (5) Ending.
  1. **Draft Construction
Outlining completed

Experiment Summary: Cognitive Interference Assessment via Stroop Paradigm

Detailed Experimental Design & Procedure:
This experiment utilizes the classic Stroop paradigm to measure cognitive interference and response inhibition. Participants view color words printed in incongruent, congruent, or neutral ink colors and must verbally or button-press the ink color while ignoring the word's semantic meaning. The design follows a within-subjects structure with counterbalanced trial order to minimize order effects.

Variables:

  • Independent Variable (IV): Congruency condition (3 levels: Congruent [e.g., "RED" in red], Incongruent [e.g., "BLUE" in red], Neutral [e.g., "XXXXX" in red])
  • Dependent Variables (DV): Reaction time (ms) to correct responses; Accuracy rate (%)
  • Controlled Variables (CV): Stimulus font size/type, screen distance, ambient lighting, stimulus onset asynchrony, practice block completion criteria, inter-trial interval consistency

Trial Structure & Sequence:

  1. Instruction for Entire Experiment: Participants receive standardized written/digital instructions explaining the task goal, response mapping, and performance expectations. A brief overview of blocks is provided.
  2. Practice Loop: 10 randomized trials covering all 3 conditions. Immediate feedback ("Correct"/"Incorrect") is shown after each trial. Loop continues until accuracy ≥80% or max attempts reached.
  3. Instruction for Main Experiment: Brief reminder of rules, emphasis on speed + accuracy balance, and confirmation that no further feedback will be given during the main block.
  4. Main Experiment Loop: 60 trials total (20 per condition). Trials are pseudo-randomized with constraints (no more than 2 identical conditions consecutively, equal distribution across halves). No feedback during this phase.
  5. Ending: Debriefing screen explaining the purpose of cognitive interference research, opportunity to ask questions, and exit prompt.

Stimulus Presentation Details:

  • Stimuli: Color words ("RED", "GREEN", "BLUE", "YELLOW") rendered in Arial Bold, 48pt, centered on a gray background (#808080). Neutral stimuli use "XXXXX".
  • Each stimulus appears centrally for a fixed duration or until response.
  • Background remains constant throughout.

Response Collection Methods:

  • Keyboard response: Keys 'F' (Red), 'J' (Green), 'K' (Blue), 'L' (Yellow) mapped to respective ink colors.
  • Response window: 2000 ms per trial. If no response, trial marked as timeout, logged, and proceeds to next trial.
  • Data recorded: Key pressed, RT from stimulus onset, correctness flag.

Loops, Conditions & Branching Logic:

  • IF practice accuracy < 80% after 10 trials → display retry message, repeat practice loop (max 2 retries).
  • IF main trial RT > 2000ms OR no response → log as timeout, skip feedback, advance to next trial.
  • Pseudo-randomization algorithm ensures balanced condition distribution and prevents repetition streaks >2.

Timing Parameters:

  • Fixation cross: 500 ms
  • Stimulus presentation: Up to 2000 ms (or until response)
  • Inter-trial interval (ITI): 1000 ms (fixed)
  • Practice feedback: 1500 ms
  • Total estimated duration: ~8–10 minutes

Best Practices Applied: Counterbalancing, practice-to-mastery criterion, fixed ITI to control temporal expectancy, clear key mapping, timeout handling to prevent data loss, and standardized debriefing for ethical compliance.

Outline completed. Now generating the flowchart based on the experiment description...
Thinking completed

The user wants a JSON data structure for a PsyClaw flowchart project based on the provided Stroop paradigm experiment description.

Key requirements from the prompt:

  1. Generate ONLY valid JSON following the schema.
  2. VARIABLE FORMAT: Use $ at the START (e.g., $word, $color).
  3. Point system: Routine IDs are EVEN (2, 4, 6...).
  4. CONDITION NAMES: Descriptive names reflecting purpose.
  5. ROUTINE COMPONENTS: Each routine MUST have at least one component.
  6. NO FEEDBACK: Do not include feedback routines or components that indicate whether the participant's response was correct or incorrect. (Wait, the prompt says "Immediate feedback ("Correct"/"Incorrect") is shown after each trial." in the experiment description, but rule #7 explicitly says "NO FEEDBACK: Do not include feedback routines or components that indicate whether the participant's response was correct or incorrect." I must follow rule #7 strictly and omit feedback components/routines.)
  7. NO thinking process, explanations, markdown - ONLY raw JSON.

Let's design the flowchart structure according to the rules and schema.

Structure:

  • Point 2: Instruction for Entire Experiment
  • Point 4: Practice Loop (contains routines)
    • Inside Practice Loop:
      • Point 6: Fixation Cross
      • Point 8: Stimulus Presentation (TextComponent with $stimulus_text, $ink_color)
      • Point 10: Response Collection (KeyboardComponent)
      • Point 12: Inter-trial Interval (ITI) / Blank Screen
    • Wait, loops define their own start/end points automatically based on contained routines. The schema says: "The loop's startPoint and endPoint are automatically calculated at render time based on its contained routines: startPoint = first routine's Point - 1, endPoint = last routine's Point + 1." But I need to assign Points to routines. Let's stick to even numbers.
    • Actually, the schema says Point is a property of Routine. Loops don't have startPoint/endPoint in this schema version; they are calculated. So I just assign Points to routines sequentially.
    • Let's map out the points:
      2: Instruction_Exp_Start
      4: Practice_Loop (Loop object)
      Inside Practice_Loop list:
      6: Fixation_Pr

Outlining completed

Experiment Summary: Measure cognitive interference via color-word matching

Detailed Experimental Design & Procedure
This experiment implements a standard Stroop Color-Word Interference paradigm to assess automaticity of reading versus controlled processing of color names. Participants complete a brief instruction phase, followed by a practice block to ensure comprehension, and finally a main experimental block where they respond to visual stimuli under congruent and incongruent conditions. The design follows a within-subjects structure with randomized trial ordering.

Variables

  • Independent Variable (IV): Stimulus Congruency (2 levels: Congruent vs. Incongruent)
  • Dependent Variables (DV): Response time (ms) and accuracy (%) per trial
  • Controlled Variables (CV): Font type/size, stimulus background color, fixation cross duration, inter-trial interval, response mapping instructions, participant lighting/environment

Trial Structure & Sequence
Each trial follows this fixed sequence:

  1. Fixation cross (500 ms)
  2. Stimulus presentation (max 2000 ms or until response)
  3. Inter-trial interval (ITI) (1000 ms blank screen)
  4. Trial repeats across blocks

Stimulus Presentation Details

  • Stimuli consist of color words ("RED", "BLUE", "GREEN", "YELLOW") printed in either matching (congruent) or mismatching (incongruent) ink colors.
  • All stimuli are displayed centrally on a neutral gray background using a standardized sans-serif font (e.g., Arial, 48pt).
  • No auditory or secondary visual cues are presented.

Response Collection Methods

  • Participants press one of four designated keys (e.g., J, K, L, ; or arrow keys) corresponding to the ink color of the word, not the word meaning.
  • Responses are recorded with millisecond precision. Incorrect responses trigger a brief "ERROR" feedback display (500 ms) before proceeding to the ITI.

Loops, Conditions & Branching Logic

  • Practice Loop: 8 trials (4 congruent, 4 incongruent), fully randomized. Ends after completion or if accuracy <70% (triggers repeat loop).
  • Main Experiment Loop: 64 trials (32 congruent, 32 incongruent), counterbalanced across participants. Trials are pseudo-randomized to prevent >2 consecutive same-congruency trials.
  • Branching: If practice accuracy <70%, the system loops back to Practice Loop. Otherwise, proceeds to Main Experiment. Post-experiment, results are saved and the Ending phase triggers.

Timing Parameters

  • Fixation: 500 ms
  • Stimulus window: 2000 ms max (auto-advance on timeout)
  • ITI: 1000 ms
  • Feedback (error only): 500 ms
  • Block transition pause: 3000 ms

Experimental Flow

  1. Instruction for Entire Experiment: Welcome screen explains task goal, response keys, and general rules.
  2. Practice Loop: 8 guided trials with immediate correctness feedback.
  3. Instruction for Main Experiment: Brief reminder of key mappings and emphasis on speed + accuracy.
  4. Main Experiment Loop: 64 randomized trials with automated logging.
  5. Ending: Thank-you screen, data export confirmation, and optional debrief prompt.
Outline completed. Now generating the flowchart based on the experiment description...

  • Response: 4 keys (e.g., J, K, L, ;) for ink color. Records RT & accuracy.
  • Feedback: "ERROR" for 500ms on incorrect response. (Note: Rule 7 says "NO FEEDBACK: Do not include feedback routines or components that indicate whether the participant's response was correct or incorrect." I must strictly follow this rule. I will remove the error feedback component as per the explicit constraint.)

    • Branching: Practice -> Main -> Ending.
  • Timing: Fixation 500ms, Stimulus 2000ms, ITI 1000ms, Block transition 3000ms.
  • Flow: Instructions -> Practice Loop -> Instructions -> Main Loop -> Ending.

  • NO thinking/ex

Outlining completed

Experiment Summary: Basic Visual Reaction Time Task

Detailed Experimental Design & Procedure
This experiment assesses participants' basic visual processing speed and motor response latency using a simple reaction time paradigm. Participants will view a central fixation cross, followed by a target stimulus (a colored circle), and press a designated key as quickly as possible upon detection. The design follows a linear progression: global instructions → practice block → main block instructions → main experimental loop → debrief/ending.

Variables

  • Independent Variable (IV): Stimulus onset delay (fixed at 1000 ms post-fixation)
  • Dependent Variable (DV): Response time (RT in milliseconds from stimulus onset to keypress) and accuracy (% correct)
  • Controlled Variables (CV): Screen brightness, stimulus size/color, response mapping (always 'Spacebar'), ambient lighting, participant distance from screen (~60 cm)

Trial Structure & Sequence
Each trial follows this exact sequence:

  1. Fixation cross (+) appears centrally for 500 ms
  2. Inter-trial interval (ITI) jittered between 800–1200 ms (randomized per trial)
  3. Target stimulus (red circle, 5° visual angle) appears
  4. Participant responds via Spacebar
  5. Feedback display (✓ or ✗) for 500 ms
  6. Loop repeats until trial completion

Stimulus Presentation Details

  • Background: #FFFFFF (white)
  • Fixation: Black +, 24pt font, centered
  • Target: Solid red circle (#FF0000), diameter 50px, centered
  • Feedback: Green checkmark or red X, 36pt, centered
  • All stimuli rendered on a single canvas with no overlapping elements

Response Collection Methods

  • Input device: Standard keyboard (Spacebar mapped as primary response)
  • Data logged: Timestamp of keypress relative to stimulus onset, key code, trial number
  • Invalid responses (e.g., pre-stimulus presses, double presses) are flagged and excluded from RT analysis but counted in accuracy metrics

Loops, Conditions & Branching Logic

  • Practice Loop: 5 trials. After each trial, displays "Correct!" or "Too early/slow." No data saved. Proceeds automatically after 5th trial.
  • Main Experiment Loop: 30 trials. Fixed order. No branching; runs sequentially. RT < 200 ms or > 2000 ms flagged as outliers.
  • Ending: Displays total mean RT, accuracy %, and thank-you message. Auto-closes after 5 seconds.

Timing Parameters

  • Fixation: 500 ms
  • ITI jitter: 800–1200 ms (uniform random)
  • Target duration: Until response or max 2000 ms
  • Feedback: 500 ms
  • Block pause: 3000 ms before main loop starts
  • Total experiment duration: ~4 minutes
